nodes.title: Return early for empty input
nodes.why: If there are no characters, there is nothing to count, so we return an empty result right away.
nodes.alternative: Let the loop run and rely on default values.
nodes.tradeoff: Clear edge-case handling with one extra line.

nodes.title: Track frequency in an object map
nodes.why: This increments each character count in O(1) average time per step.
nodes.alternative: Use a `Map` instead of a plain object.
nodes.tradeoff: Simple and fast for this case, but object keys are always strings.

nodes.title: Update current best during traversal
nodes.why: We keep `max` and `maxChar` up to date as we go, so no second pass is needed.
nodes.alternative: First build counts, then loop again to find the maximum.
nodes.tradeoff: More efficient in one pass, with slightly more logic inside the main loop.

nodes.title: Use `reduce` to build frequency table
nodes.why: `reduce` collects all counts into one object in a concise functional style.
nodes.alternative: Use a regular `for...of` loop to build the same map.
nodes.tradeoff: Compact expression, but less explicit for beginners.

nodes.title: Scan frequency entries to find max
nodes.why: Each `[character, count]` pair is compared to keep track of the most frequent one.
nodes.alternative: Sort entries by count and take the first element.
nodes.tradeoff: Linear scan is efficient and simple, while sorting costs extra work.

nodes.title: Update winner when count is higher
nodes.why: When a larger count appears, we immediately store both the new max and its character.
nodes.alternative: Track only max count, then search again for matching character.
nodes.tradeoff: Single pass over entries, with small mutable state variables.
